title: Medhavy: the AI-Tutor Layer via LTI

segments.label: 01 / 12 · HOOK
title: SEAMLESS ENTRY
NO SECOND LOGIN.
segments.body_html:
Canvas Link Click→Full Context→Authenticated

A student opens Canvas, clicks a Medhavy link in Chapter 4, and lands inside a fully-contextualized tutoring session. No login screen. No account creation. The tutor already knows her course, her progress through prior chapters, and exactly where she is in the arc.

beats.text: The opening image of this chapter is a student in Canvas, in the module for Chapter 4. She sees familiar items — a page, a quiz, a case study — and a link labeled Chapter 4 Tutor. She clicks it. A new tab opens. She does not see a login screen. She does not see create an account. She is already inside Medhavy, already authenticated, and the tutor already knows her course enrollment, her completion of Chapters 1 through 3, and that she is now on Chapter 4. The tutor's first message is not Hi, I'm an AI tutor, what would you like to learn? It is specific to where she is in the arc of the book. That seamless entry — clicking a link and landing inside a fully contextualized session without a second login — is LTI 1.3 doing the work you cannot do by hand.

segments.label: 02 / 12 · THE STAKES
title: THIS CHAPTER REQUIRES
INSTITUTIONAL APPARATUS.
segments.body_html:
Chapters 16–18Artifacts you built · Scripts you ran · Packages you deployed
⇔
Chapter 19Specification you hand to developers · Registration with Canvas admin · FERPA review · IT security review

Chapters 16, 17, and 18 each ended with an artifact you made and shipped yourself. Chapter 19 ends with a conversation — a specification for developers and your institution. Medhavy is separately hosted. LTI registration involves institutional approval you cannot automate.

beats.text: This chapter is different from Chapters 16, 17, and 18. Say that plainly because it matters. Chapter 16 asked you to run a script that produced an .apkg file for Anki. Chapter 17 asked you to run a script that produced an .imscc package you uploaded to Canvas. Chapter 18 asked you to run a script that scaffolded a React site you deployed to Vercel. Each of those chapters ended with you holding an artifact you made. Chapter 19 ends with a conversation — a specification you hand to a developer and to your institution. Medhavy is a separately hosted system. LTI 1.3 is a protocol that requires registration with your institution's Canvas administrator. The connection between Canvas and Medhavy involves a FERPA review, an IT security review, and institutional approval that no script can automate. This chapter teaches you what is being built and why, so you can specify it accurately and evaluate it honestly when it is done. You will not build it alone.

segments.label: 03 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: LTI 1.3 IS
THE PROTOCOL.
segments.body_html:
LTILearning Tools Interoperability — the standard protocol for connecting external tools to learning management systems
AuthenticatedUses OAuth 2.0-based OIDC flow, replacing earlier shared-secret model
Version 1.3Published by IMS Global Learning Consortium, now 1EdTech

LTI 1.3 makes Medhavy and Canvas speak the same language while preserving privacy and delegating authentication to Canvas as the identity provider.

beats.text: LTI stands for Learning Tools Interoperability. It is the standard protocol for connecting an external tool — Medhavy — to a learning management system — Canvas — in a way that is authenticated, authorized, and privacy-aware. Version 1.3, published by the IMS Global Learning Consortium, now called 1EdTech, replaced the earlier shared-secret model with an OAuth 2.0-based OIDC flow. The mechanism matters enough to name even if you never touch the code. OIDC is OpenID Connect, an identity layer built on OAuth 2.0. The key move is this: instead of Medhavy and Canvas sharing a secret they both use to sign messages, Canvas signs a token with its own private key. Medhavy verifies that signature using Canvas's public key. This is more secure, auditable, and aligned with modern identity practice. What it means for you: no separate Medhavy account creation, no password for students, no copy-paste of enrollment data. Canvas is the identity provider. Medhavy trusts it.

segments.label: 04 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: THE OAUTH HANDSHAKE
TAKES ONE SECOND.
segments.body_html:
Canvas Sends JWT→Medhavy Verifies Signature→Student Lands

The JWT carries claims: user identity, course enrollment, module context, role. Medhavy verifies the signature against Canvas's published JWKS and completes the handshake invisibly. The student sees none of this machinery.

beats.text: When the student clicks the Medhavy link in Canvas, Canvas does not simply redirect her to a URL. It initiates an OIDC login flow. Canvas passes a signed JWT — a JSON Web Token — to Medhavy's login endpoint, containing claims about who the user is, which course she is in, which module item she clicked, and what role she holds: student, instructor, or TA. Medhavy receives this token and verifies the JWT's signature against Canvas's published JWKS — JSON Web Key Set — which is publicly available. If the signature checks out, Medhavy knows the token came from Canvas and has not been tampered with. Then Medhavy completes the handshake and logs the student in. The whole exchange takes less than a second. The student sees none of it. She just lands inside Medhavy with full context already populated. No login screen. No password. No wait.

segments.label: 05 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: NRPS PULLS THE
ROSTER FROM CANVAS.
segments.body_html:
Names and Role Provisioning ServicesFirst LTI Advantage service — pulls live course roster without CSV export
Medhavy AnalyticsDashboard shows engagement by student, knows who is and is not engaging
Instructor VisibilitySee which students completed which activities, all in one place

NRPS makes the analytics dashboard real-time and keeps enrollment synchronized automatically between Canvas and Medhavy.

beats.text: Three sub-protocols extend base LTI 1.3 and together make Medhavy's deeper integrations possible. These are called LTI Advantage services, and you do not implement them — you specify that your deployment should use them. The first is Names and Role Provisioning Services, NRPS. NRPS lets Medhavy pull the current course roster from Canvas — names, roles, enrollment status — without you exporting a CSV file. This happens automatically. Medhavy can ask Canvas: who is enrolled in this course? What role does each student have? When did they enroll? Canvas answers via API. This is how the Medhavy analytics dashboard knows who is and is not engaging with the tutor. This is how an instructor can log into Medhavy and see a list of students with completion status for each activity. The enrollment stays in sync without manual intervention.

segments.label: 06 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: DEEP LINKING IS
THE CONTENT PICKER.
segments.body_html:
Content PickerOpens inside Canvas module editor when you add an external tool
No Hard-Coded URLsClick and choose from Medhavy items instead of copying URLs by hand
Canvas Stores the LinkDeep Linking returns a reference that Canvas manages as a module item

Deep Linking removes copy-paste friction and makes Medhavy items feel native to the Canvas module editor.

beats.text: The second LTI Advantage service is Deep Linking. Deep Linking lets Medhavy offer a content picker inside Canvas's module editor. When you build a Canvas module and want to add a Medhavy chapter tutor, formative activity, or assessment, you click Add External Tool, select Medhavy, and a picker dialog opens inside your browser. The picker shows available Medhavy items — Chapter 4 Tutor, Quiz 4A, Interactive Case Study 4, and so on. You click the one you want. Canvas stores a reference to that item. The next time a student clicks that module item, Canvas initiates the LTI handshake with that specific Medhavy context built in. This is what makes it possible to add Medhavy items to Canvas without hard-coding URLs by hand or managing a spreadsheet of links. Deep Linking makes the integration feel native.

segments.label: 07 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: AGS WRITES GRADES
BACK TO CANVAS.
segments.body_html:
Student Completes Activity→Medhavy Posts Score→Appears in Gradebook

AGS synchronizes learning outcomes back to Canvas without duplicating records. One transaction, two authoritative records: Canvas owns the grade line-item, Medhavy owns the learning data.

beats.text: The third LTI Advantage service is Assignment and Grade Services, AGS. AGS lets Medhavy write grade and completion data back to the Canvas gradebook. When a student finishes a Medhavy activity — say, a chapter tutor session or formative quiz — Medhavy posts a score or completion status to Canvas through the AGS API. That score appears in the Canvas gradebook as if the student had submitted a Canvas quiz. This creates a single, unified gradebook view. The student does not track two separate grade records. But here is the key: this is not duplication. Medhavy posts the grade to Canvas, Canvas displays it. Canvas owns the official grade record. Medhavy owns the full interaction history — what the student asked, what the tutor said, how many attempts, where comprehension broke down. This is the split we are about to make very clear.

segments.label: 08 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: CANVAS AND MEDHAVY
ARE NOT COPIES.
segments.body_html:
Canvas OwnsEnrollment · Official gradebook · Assignment deadlines · Course communications · Institutional identity
Medhavy OwnsAI-tutor interaction history · Learning activity within the textbook · Chapter-level reading progress · Formative activity attempts · Learning analytics

Getting this split clear is the architectural decision that prevents future confusion. These are authoritative records of different things.

beats.text: Canvas and Medhavy are not competing records of the same thing. They are authoritative records of different things. Getting this split clear is the architectural decision that prevents future confusion. Canvas owns enrollment, official gradebook, assignment deadlines, course communications, institutional identity. Canvas is where grades are official, enrollment is canonical, institutional deadlines are enforced. Medhavy owns AI-tutor interaction history, learning activity within the textbook, chapter-level reading progress, formative activity attempts, learning analytics at session and course level. Medhavy is where the student's learning conversation with the tutor is preserved and analyzed. When a student completes a Medhavy activity, AGS writes a line-item score to Canvas. Canvas stores the grade. Medhavy stores the full interaction — what the student asked, what the tutor said, how many attempts, where comprehension broke down — in its own database, governed by its own privacy policy.

segments.label: 09 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: MEDHAVY STORES THE
LEARNING RECORD.
segments.body_html:
Full Interaction HistoryEvery question the student asked, every response the tutor gave, every attempt
Comprehension SignalsWhere understanding broke down, where reteaching happened, pace of mastery
Privacy GovernanceStored in Medhavy's database, governed by Medhavy's privacy policy

You will look at Canvas for the gradebook picture and look at the Medhavy analytics dashboard for the learning picture.

beats.text: This separation of concern has profound implications for how you understand your students. When you want to know a student's grade, you look at the Canvas gradebook. Canvas tells you: this student earned 87% on Chapter 4 formative activities. When you want to understand how the student learned and where they struggled, you go to Medhavy. Medhavy tells you: this student asked seventeen questions about causality, mostly in the third section. The tutor rephrased the definition four times. On the fifth rephrase, using an analogy to a lever, the student's subsequent questions showed comprehension had occurred. The student revisited the concept once more two days later, comfortable. One record shows what was earned. The other shows how it was learned. A student can have a complete gradebook record and reveal only partial learning in Medhavy, or vice versa — they are independent measurements. Together, they form a complete picture. Separately, they tell different stories.

segments.label: 10 / 12 · SYNTHESIS
title: TWO DASHBOARDS
TWO COMPLETE TRUTHS.
segments.body_html:
Canvas GradebookInstitutional view: who passed, who's behind, grade distribution, class cohort progress
Medhavy AnalyticsLearning view: where students struggled, how tutoring progressed, what concepts need reteaching

Neither dashboard is complete without the other. Together they form the full picture of what was earned and how it was learned.

beats.text: The instructor experience is fundamentally dual. You log into Canvas and see the gradebook: the official record of what each student earned, what the distribution looks like, who is on track and who is at risk. This is institutional truth — the basis for grade decisions, identifying at-risk students, demonstrating learning outcomes. You log into Medhavy and see a different dashboard: session-by-session engagement, question types that generated confusion, how many attempts each activity required, patterns in concept revisits. This is learning truth — the basis for pacing decisions, reteaching choices, which examples to emphasize, where to scaffold more carefully. Neither is sufficient alone. Canvas without Medhavy gives grades but no insight into process. Medhavy without Canvas gives process but no official record. Together, they form a complete understanding of student learning. The mechanism making this coexistence possible — without compromising either system's authority or privacy — is precisely the architecture LTI 1.3 and its Advantage services provide: separate but integrated, trusting but independent.

segments.label: 11 / 12 · THESIS
title: LTI BRIDGES INSTITUTIONAL
IDENTITY AND LEARNING.
segments.body_html:
The mechanism that makes dual record-keeping possible is LTI 1.3 — an OAuth-based protocol that trusts Canvas as identity provider, then leaves Medhavy free to be its own learning record.

The split of authority is not a limitation; it is the architecture that makes independent, privacy-respecting record-keeping possible at scale. Canvas is the map. Medhavy is the territory. Together they answer both what and how.

beats.text: The mechanism that makes this possible is LTI 1.3 — an OAuth-based authentication protocol that accomplishes something subtle and powerful. It lets Canvas say to Medhavy: I authenticate this user. Medhavy trusts that claim. Then it lets Medhavy say to Canvas: I have a grade to record. Canvas receives it. But Canvas never needs to know what learning happened in Medhavy, and Medhavy never manages enrollment or institutional identity. Each system does one thing well and trusts the other for the rest. The split of authority is not a limitation; it is the architecture that makes independent, privacy-respecting record-keeping possible at scale. The chapter opened with this metaphor: the Canvas course is the map. Medhavy is the conversation that happens when the student walks into the territory. The map tells you where to go. The conversation tells you what the student understands about the journey. LTI 1.3 is the bridge between them — a protocol that keeps them separate enough to preserve each system's authority, yet integrated enough to answer the full question: what did the student learn, and how did they learn it?

segments.label: 12 / 12 · CLOSE
title: THE AI-TUTOR LAYER
VIA LTI.
segments.body_html:
LTI 1.3//OAUTH 2.0 OIDC//MEDHAVY

Series · Chapter 19 · Medhavy: the AI-Tutor Layer via LTI

beats.text: This is the architecture that brings an AI tutor into your course without compromising Canvas's institutional authority or Medhavy's independence. LTI 1.3 makes both systems possible.
